title: Chapter 9: Centre of Mass and Collision - Study Notes
source: A Quick Review of E-PCM for Engineering Entrance - Physics

sections.heading: Centre of Mass Basics
sections.content: Centre of mass depends on mass distribution of the system. If a body is symmetric about a point, line, or axis, then its centre of mass lies on that symmetry element.
formulas: For n particles: r_cm = (m1*r1 + m2*r2 + ... + mn*rn)/(m1 + m2 + ... + mn)
For continuous body: r_cm = (integral r dm)/(integral dm)
x_cm = (sum m_i x_i)/(sum m_i), y_cm = (sum m_i y_i)/(sum m_i), z_cm = (sum m_i z_i)/(sum m_i)
specialCases: Centre of mass of a system is independent of internal forces.
External force decides motion of centre of mass.

sections.heading: Velocity and Acceleration of COM
sections.content: Velocity and acceleration of the centre of mass are weighted averages of individual velocities and accelerations.
formulas: F_ext = M*a_cm
specialCases: If net external force is zero, linear momentum of system is constant.
Then velocity of centre of mass remains constant.

sections.heading: Collision Fundamentals
sections.content: During collision, linear momentum is always conserved for an isolated system. Coefficient of restitution decides elasticity.
formulas: e = (relative speed of separation)/(relative speed of approach) = (v2 - v1)/(u1 - u2)
Perfectly elastic collision: e = 1
Inelastic collision: 0 < e < 1
Perfectly inelastic collision: e = 0
specialCases: Elastic collision conserves both momentum and kinetic energy.
Inelastic collision conserves momentum but not kinetic energy.

sections.heading: Head-On Elastic Collision Results
sections.content: For one-dimensional (head-on) elastic collision, two equations are used: momentum conservation and restitution condition with e = 1.
formulas: If m1 = m2: velocities are interchanged
If m1 = m2 and u2 = 0: v1 = 0 and v2 = u1
If lighter mass hits heavier mass at rest: lighter may rebound
If heavier mass hits lighter mass at rest: lighter speed can be nearly twice heavier speed
specialCases: Relative speed relation is often fastest way to solve objective questions.
Kinetic energy loss is zero for elastic case.

sections.heading: Head-On Inelastic Collision and Common Forms
sections.content: For inelastic collision, momentum is conserved but relative speed relation includes e < 1.
formulas: For identical masses, one moving and one at rest: v1:v2 = (1-e):(1+e)
For identical masses, moving mass ratio after/before = (1-e)/2
specialCases: For perfectly inelastic collision, common final velocity v = (m1*u1 + m2*u2)/(m1 + m2).
Maximum kinetic energy loss occurs in perfectly inelastic collision for given initial state.

sections.heading: Rebound and Explosion Applications
sections.content: For repeated bounces and explosions, momentum/energy methods give compact results used in exams.
formulas: Ball dropped from height h with restitution e: rebound height after n-th bounce h_n = e^(2n)*h
Time after n-th bounce: t_n = e^n * t (if initial descent time is t)
Total ascent height = (h*e^2)/(1-e^2)
Total descent distance = h*(1+e^2)/(1-e^2)
Explosion at rest: vector sum of fragment momenta = 0
specialCases: Rocket propulsion is based on conservation of momentum/Newton third law.
For internal explosion, centre of mass continues original motion if no external force acts.
